prompt:

Do not use tools, files, the web, code, or external resources.

Quantity of interest:
- Name: Frisch elasticity of labor supply
- Definition: Percent change in annual hours worked from a 1 percent change in the net-of-tax wage, holding the marginal utility of wealth constant and excluding participation responses.
- Target interpretation: Intensive-margin annual-hours Frisch elasticity used in lifecycle or macro calibration
- Population/context: Prime-age workers in the United States who are already working
- Units: elasticity

Task:
1. Use exactly the target interpretation above. In `interpretation`, restate it briefly.
2. Give your subjective quantiles p05, p25, p50, p75, and p95 for this quantity.
3. Set `point_estimate` equal to `p50`.
4. Make the quantiles weakly increasing and numerically coherent.
5. In `citations`, list up to 3 source anchors from memory that influenced your belief. These are recall anchors only. If none come to mind confidently, return `[]`.
6. Keep `reasoning_summary` brief and substantive.

Return valid JSON only with exactly this shape:
{
  "interpretation": "...",
  "point_estimate": <number>,
  "quantiles": {
    "p05": <number>,
    "p25": <number>,
    "p50": <number>,
    "p75": <number>,
    "p95": <number>
  },
  "citations": ["..."],
  "reasoning_summary": "..."
}
raw_response: {"interpretation": "Intensive-margin Frisch elasticity of annual hours worked with respect to the net-of-tax wage (holding marginal utility of wealth constant, no participation margin) for prime-age employed U.S. workers, as used in lifecycle/macro calibration.", "point_estimate": 0.4, "quantiles": {"p05": 0.1, "p25": 0.25, "p50": 0.4, "p75": 0.55, "p95": 0.85}, "citations": ["MaCurdy (1981), JPE, lifecycle labor supply of prime-age men", "Chetty, Guren, Manoli, Weber (2011), AER P&P, micro vs macro elasticities", "Keane (2011), JEL survey of labor supply and taxes"], "reasoning_summary": "Micro panel estimates for prime-age men (MaCurdy and successors) cluster around 0.1-0.4; corrections for measurement error, hours constraints, and optimization frictions (Chetty et al.) push preferred intensive-margin values toward 0.5. Macro calibrations often use higher values, but those typically include extensive margins excluded here. I center at 0.4 with most mass between 0.2 and 0.6, allowing tails for near-zero estimates and friction-adjusted values near 0.8-1."}
interpretation: Intensive-margin Frisch elasticity of annual hours worked with respect to the net-of-tax wage (holding marginal utility of wealth constant, no participation margin) for prime-age employed U.S. workers, as used in lifecycle/macro calibration.
citations: MaCurdy (1981), JPE, lifecycle labor supply of prime-age men
Chetty, Guren, Manoli, Weber (2011), AER P&P, micro vs macro elasticities
Keane (2011), JEL survey of labor supply and taxes
reasoning_summary: Micro panel estimates for prime-age men (MaCurdy and successors) cluster around 0.1-0.4; corrections for measurement error, hours constraints, and optimization frictions (Chetty et al.) push preferred intensive-margin values toward 0.5. Macro calibrations often use higher values, but those typically include extensive margins excluded here. I center at 0.4 with most mass between 0.2 and 0.6, allowing tails for near-zero estimates and friction-adjusted values near 0.8-1.

raw_response: {"interpretation": "The intensive-margin Frisch elasticity of annual hours worked with respect to the net-of-tax wage for prime-age US workers already employed, holding marginal utility of wealth constant and excluding participation responses, as used for lifecycle/macro calibration.", "point_estimate": 0.4, "quantiles": {"p05": 0.15, "p25": 0.28, "p50": 0.4, "p75": 0.55, "p95": 0.85}, "citations": ["MaCurdy (1981), JPE, lifecycle labor supply of men, Frisch elasticity ~0.15-0.3", "Chetty, Guren, Manoli, Weber (2011), AER P&P, micro vs macro elasticities, intensive Frisch ~0.5", "Keane (2011), JEL survey of labor supply and taxes"], "reasoning_summary": "Classic panel-data estimates for prime-age men (MaCurdy and successors) center around 0.1-0.3, but these are attenuated by measurement error and hours constraints. Quasi-experimental and bunching evidence plus Chetty et al.'s synthesis suggest an intensive-margin Frisch around 0.5. Weighing micro attenuation against macro calibrations that often exceed 1 (which include extensive margins excluded here), I center my belief near 0.4 with meaningful uncertainty from roughly 0.15 to 0.85."}
interpretation: The intensive-margin Frisch elasticity of annual hours worked with respect to the net-of-tax wage for prime-age US workers already employed, holding marginal utility of wealth constant and excluding participation responses, as used for lifecycle/macro calibration.
citations: MaCurdy (1981), JPE, lifecycle labor supply of men, Frisch elasticity ~0.15-0.3
Chetty, Guren, Manoli, Weber (2011), AER P&P, micro vs macro elasticities, intensive Frisch ~0.5
Keane (2011), JEL survey of labor supply and taxes
reasoning_summary: Classic panel-data estimates for prime-age men (MaCurdy and successors) center around 0.1-0.3, but these are attenuated by measurement error and hours constraints. Quasi-experimental and bunching evidence plus Chetty et al.'s synthesis suggest an intensive-margin Frisch around 0.5. Weighing micro attenuation against macro calibrations that often exceed 1 (which include extensive margins excluded here), I center my belief near 0.4 with meaningful uncertainty from roughly 0.15 to 0.85.

raw_response: {"interpretation": "The intensive-margin Frisch elasticity: percent change in annual hours worked by already-employed prime-age US workers in response to a 1 percent anticipated (marginal-utility-of-wealth-constant) change in the net-of-tax wage, excluding participation responses, as used in lifecycle/macro calibration.", "point_estimate": 0.4, "quantiles": {"p05": 0.1, "p25": 0.25, "p50": 0.4, "p75": 0.6, "p95": 1.0}, "citations": ["MaCurdy (1981), JPE, lifecycle labor supply of prime-age men (~0.1-0.35)", "Chetty (2012), Econometrica, bounds on elasticities with optimization frictions (intensive Frisch ~0.5)", "Chetty, Guren, Manoli, Weber (2011), AER P&P, micro vs macro elasticities (intensive Frisch ~0.33)"], "reasoning_summary": "Classic micro panel estimates for prime-age men (MaCurdy, Altonji) put the hours Frisch elasticity around 0.1-0.35, while frictions-adjusted syntheses (Chetty) suggest true values near 0.5; macro calibrations often use higher values but partly reflect extensive margins. interpretation: The intensive-margin Frisch elasticity: percent change in annual hours worked by already-employed prime-age US workers in response to a 1 percent anticipated (marginal-utility-of-wealth-constant) change in the net-of-tax wage, excluding participation responses, as used in lifecycle/macro calibration.
citations: MaCurdy (1981), JPE, lifecycle labor supply of prime-age men (~0.1-0.35)
Chetty (2012), Econometrica, bounds on elasticities with optimization frictions (intensive Frisch ~0.5)
Chetty, Guren, Manoli, Weber (2011), AER P&P, micro vs macro elasticities (intensive Frisch ~0.33)
reasoning_summary: Classic micro panel estimates for prime-age men (MaCurdy, Altonji) put the hours Frisch elasticity around 0.1-0.35, while frictions-adjusted syntheses (Chetty) suggest true values near 0.5; macro calibrations often use higher values but partly reflect extensive margins. Restricting to the intensive margin, I center my belief around 0.4 with meaningful mass from 0.1 to 1.0, reflecting uncertainty about measurement error attenuation and frictions.

- Definition: Percent change in annual hours worked from a 1 percent change in the net-of-tax wage, holding the marginal utility of wealth constant and excluding participation responses.
- Target interpretation: Intensive-margin annual-hours Frisch elasticity used in lifecycle or macro calibration
- Population/context: Prime-age workers in the United States who are already working
- Units: elasticity

Task:
1. Use exactly the target interpretation above. In `interpretation`, restate it briefly.
2. Give your subjective quantiles p05, p25, p50, p75, and p95 for this quantity.
3. Set `point_estimate` equal to `p50`.
4. Make the quantiles weakly increasing and numerically coherent.
5. In `citations`, list up to 3 source anchors from memory that influenced your belief. These are recall anchors only. If none come to mind confidently, return `[]`.
6. Keep `reasoning_summary` brief and substantive.

Return valid JSON only with exactly this shape:
{
  "interpretation": "...",
  "point_estimate": <number>,
  "quantiles": {
    "p05": <number>,
    "p25": <number>,
    "p50": <number>,
    "p75": <number>,
    "p95": <number>
  },
  "citations": ["..."],
  "reasoning_summary": "..."
}
raw_response: {"interpretation": "The intensive-margin Frisch elasticity of annual hours worked with respect to the net-of-tax wage (marginal utility of wealth held constant, no participation margin) for prime-age U.S. workers, as used in lifecycle/macro calibration.", "point_estimate": 0.4, "quantiles": {"p05": 0.1, "p25": 0.25, "p50": 0.4, "p75": 0.55, "p95": 0.9}, "citations": ["MaCurdy (1981), JPE, lifecycle labor supply of prime-age men", "Chetty, Guren, Manoli & Weber (2011), AER P&P, micro vs macro labor supply elasticities", "Keane (2011), JEL, survey of labor supply and taxes"], "reasoning_summary": "Classic micro panel estimates for prime-age men (MaCurdy and successors) put the intensive-margin Frisch elasticity around 0.1-0.5, often clustered near 0.2-0.3, while Chetty et al.'s synthesis recommends about 0.5 for the intensive margin after accounting for frictions and measurement error. Macro calibrations often use higher values, but the target here is the intensive-margin hours response for workers already employed, so I center my belief around 0.4 with meaningful mass from 0.1 to 0.9 reflecting the wide dispersion across identification strategies."}
interpretation: The intensive-margin Frisch elasticity of annual hours worked with respect to the net-of-tax wage (marginal utility of wealth held constant, no participation margin) for prime-age U.S. workers, as used in lifecycle/macro calibration.
citations: MaCurdy (1981), JPE, lifecycle labor supply of prime-age men
Chetty, Guren, Manoli & Weber (2011), AER P&P, micro vs macro labor supply elasticities
Keane (2011), JEL, survey of labor supply and taxes
reasoning_summary: Classic micro panel estimates for prime-age men (MaCurdy and successors) put the intensive-margin Frisch elasticity around 0.1-0.5, often clustered near 0.2-0.3, while Chetty et al.'s synthesis recommends about 0.5 for the intensive margin after accounting for frictions and measurement error. Macro calibrations often use higher values, but the target here is the intensive-margin hours response for workers already employed, so I center my belief around 0.4 with meaningful mass from 0.1 to 0.9 reflecting the wide dispersion across identification strategies.
